title: Malaysian Laksa (Coconut Noodle Soup)

ingredients.name: rice noodles (or flat rice vermicelli)
visual_checks: soak in warm water 15 minutes if dried, or use fresh
ingredients.notes: soak in warm water 15 minutes if dried, or use fresh

subs.name: 200 g chicken breast, sliced, for a non-pescatarian version
subs.notes: simmer chicken 8 minutes until cooked through

subs.name: 200 ml coconut milk powder dissolved in 300 ml water
1 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
subs.notes: changes the flavour profile slightly but works

subs.name: sliced cabbage or any crunchy raw vegetable for garnish

steps.text: If using dried rice noodles, soak them in warm water for 15 minutes until just pliable but not fully soft. Drain and set aside.
Blend onion, garlic, ginger, lemongrass and red chilli powder with 3 tbsp water into a smooth laksa paste.
Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pot on medium heat. Add the laksa paste and fry, stirring, for 6–8 minutes until the paste deepens in colour and the oil separates to the edges.

Add turmeric and kaffir lime leaves. Pour in 600 ml water and bring to a simmer. Add prawn shells and heads if you have them and simmer for 10 minutes to build a seafood stock base. Strain out the solids.
Add coconut milk and fish sauce to the strained broth. Bring to a gentle simmer — do not boil vigorously after coconut milk is in or it can split. Taste and adjust salt and fish sauce.
Add the peeled prawn and tofu to the simmering broth. Cook for 2–3 minutes until the prawn turn pink and curl. Do not overcook.

Divide the soaked noodles into bowls. Ladle the hot laksa broth with prawn and tofu over the noodles. Top with fresh bean sprouts, mint leaves and a wedge of lemon on the side. Eat immediately while piping hot.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: prawn → 200 g chicken breast, sliced, for a non-pescatarian version (simmer chicken 8 minutes until cooked through); fish-sauce → 1 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt (changes the flavour profile slightly but works); coconut-milk → 200 ml coconut milk powder dissolved in 300 ml water (reduce simmer time by 2 minutes); sprouts → sliced cabbage or any crunchy raw vegetable for garnish
indian_kitchen_adaptation: The laksa paste is built from everyday Indian ingredients: ginger (as galangal substitute), lemongrass (available at city supermarkets), red chilli powder and garlic. Use rice noodles (semiya-style thin noodles or flat rice noodles from any south Indian store). Fish sauce is increasingly available; soy sauce is a workable substitute. The heavy-bottomed pot can be a pressure-cooker base or any thick kadai.
